data.title: Jushi Holdings Earnings Call Balances Progress and Pressure
data.description:
Jushi Holdings Inc ((TSE:JUSH)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Jushi Holdings’ latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone as management emphasized operational gains and margin expansion against a backdrop of modest revenue growth and heavy debt costs. Executives highlighted improved yields, better product quality and a non‑dilutive refinancing, while acknowledging persistent pricing pressure, larger net losses and reliance on tax credits to bolster adjusted EBITDA.

Revenue Growth (Q4 and Full Year)

Jushi reported Q4 2025 revenue of $68.3 million, up roughly 4% from $65.9 million a year earlier, with full‑year sales reaching $262.9 million for a little over 2% growth. Management framed this as evidence of stabilization across its retail footprint, supported by contributions from newly opened stores in key markets.

Improved Gross Profit and Q4 Margin Expansion

Fourth‑quarter gross profit rose to $28.6 million, or 41.9% of revenue, versus $25.4 million and a 38.6% margin in Q4 2024. The company credited higher production volumes, better product quality and operational gains at its grower‑processor facilities for the improved profitability in the quarter.

Material Adjusted EBITDA Improvement

Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 climbed to $13.9 million, a 20.4% margin, compared with $8.0 million and a 12.2% margin in the prior‑year period. For 2025, adjusted EBITDA increased to $50.3 million from $46.2 million, with the full‑year margin expanding to 19.1% from 17.9%.

Operational Turnaround at Grower-Processor Facilities

Management emphasized a meaningful turnaround in cultivation, as average yield per square foot increased about 28% year over year in Q4 while maintaining potency in the mid‑ to upper‑20% THCa range. These improvements supported a more favorable product mix across both retail and wholesale channels, helping underpin margin gains.

Strategic Retail Growth and Branded Mix

The company expanded its retail footprint from 38 to 42 stores, an increase of roughly 11% year over year, while keeping headcount growth to about 4%, showcasing scaling efficiency. Jushi‑branded products accounted for around 58% of retail revenue in Q4, up from 55% a year earlier, supporting brand strength and vertical integration benefits.

Refinancing Strengthens Near-Term Liquidity

Jushi completed a $160 million first‑lien secured term loan due 2029 with a 12.5% coupon, using proceeds to repay a roughly $46 million term loan and about $86 million of second‑lien notes. The transaction added approximately $13 million of incremental liquidity, extended maturities and avoided equity dilution, with the CEO increasing his personal capital participation.

Regulatory Progress – Virginia Adult Use

Executives highlighted that Virginia’s adult‑use legislation has been reconciled and sent to the governor, mapping a path to legal sales in 2027. Under the current timeline, applications are expected by September 1, 2026, licenses by December 1, 2026 and retail adult‑use sales starting January 1, 2027, with Jushi planning to leverage existing infrastructure with limited new capital.

Selective CapEx Discipline and 2026 Guidance

Capital expenditures in 2025 totaled $16.1 million, split between $4.8 million of maintenance and $11.3 million of growth spending. For 2026, the company guided to a lower $9–13 million CapEx budget, with $4–5 million for maintenance and $5–8 million for growth, underscoring disciplined allocation tied to regulatory visibility.

Modest Top-Line Growth Overall

Despite positive commentary on operations, the company’s top line advanced only modestly, with full‑year revenue up just over 2% and Q4 revenue up about 4% compared with 2024. Management acknowledged that competitive, price‑constrained markets are limiting headline growth even as internal efficiencies improve.

Full-Year Gross Margin Compression

For 2025 as a whole, gross profit slipped to $114.0 million, or 43.4% of revenue, from $118.3 million and a 45.9% margin the prior year. The decline reflects increased promotional activity and sustained pricing pressure across several markets, partially offsetting operational gains in cultivation.

Net Loss and High Interest Expense

The company’s Q4 net loss widened to $15.6 million from $12.5 million a year earlier, with interest expense in the quarter reaching $10.4 million. Jushi ended the year with about $193.1 million of debt and the new 12.5% term loan underscores that its funding costs remain elevated despite improved liquidity.

Adjusted EBITDA Benefited from One-Time Credits

Management noted that adjusted EBITDA results were boosted by roughly $3 million of employee retention credits in Q4 and around $10.6 million recognized for the full year. These non‑recurring credits partially inflated year‑over‑year operating comparisons, tempering the apparent strength of the EBITDA improvement.

Liquidity and Cash Levels

At December 31, 2025, Jushi held about $26.6 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash, providing a modest cushion alongside the new term loan. However, the financing includes a covenant requiring the company to maintain a minimum cash balance, which limits flexibility to deploy cash aggressively.

Market Headwinds and Promotional Pressure

Management described ongoing pricing pressure and competitive dynamics across multiple markets, which are forcing deeper promotional activity, especially around holiday periods. These discounting strategies help defend market share but weigh on margins, underscoring the challenging retail backdrop.

Wholesale and Market-Specific Weaknesses

Wholesale revenue posted only modest gains, rising to $7.9 million from $7.7 million sequentially, with particular weakness in Virginia where wholesale sales fell by roughly $1.2 million as partners prioritized their own vertical products. The company also terminated a planned Mt. Laurel, N.J. location, reflecting selective retrenchment in certain markets.

Potential Large Conversion Fee and Regulatory Uncertainty

Under current Virginia legislation, converted medical licenses would owe a $10 million conversion fee, potentially payable under a structured plan, representing a significant future cash outlay. Management also flagged broader regulatory uncertainty around federal rescheduling, hemp rule implementation and capital market reforms that could materially impact the industry.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, Jushi expects 2026 CapEx to remain contained at $9–13 million, with most major construction, including larger Virginia build‑outs, deferred to 2027 and likely funded via mortgage or facility‑level debt. Management anticipates Virginia adult‑use sales beginning in 2027 and pointed to prior transitions, such as New Jersey’s roughly 3.2x uplift, as a rough benchmark for the potential impact on that market.

Jushi’s earnings call painted a picture of a company making tangible operational strides and shoring up its balance sheet, yet still grappling with thin growth, high interest costs and intense market competition. For investors, the story hinges on whether disciplined spending, improving margins and the eventual Virginia adult‑use launch can outweigh current headwinds and convert cautious optimism into durable earnings growth.

data.title: Spar Group Charts Turnaround Amid Deepening Losses
data.description:
Spar Group ((SGRP)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Spar Group’s latest earnings call painted a story of sharp short‑term pain set against an ambitious turnaround plan. Executives acknowledged heavy margin compression, sizable losses and cash burn, yet argued that a focused North American strategy, new technology, and a key retail partnership can restore growth and profitability over the next year and beyond.

North American Revenue Growth Amid Tough Backdrop

Fiscal 2025 net revenues in the U.S. and Canada rose to $136.1 million, a 3.3% increase from 2024 despite a challenging operating environment. U.S. revenue grew 3.9% to $122.1 million while Canada was roughly flat at $14.1 million, underscoring that the core market is expanding but still not offsetting margin and cost pressures.

Strategic Repositioning and Streamlined Operations

Management highlighted a completed exit from international joint ventures, leaving Spar focused squarely on the U.S. and Canada. The company rebuilt its leadership team, cut management layers and rightsized its cost base, aiming to create a leaner, more scalable business aligned with merchandising and on‑demand services rather than a sprawling global footprint.

ReposiTrak Partnership Targets Data‑Driven Execution

A major theme was a new, live strategic partnership with ReposiTrak that joins shelf out‑of‑stock detection, route optimization and Spar’s surge merchandising force. The goal is a data‑driven “signal to fix” loop where store issues are identified digitally and corrected quickly in the aisle, an offering now being marketed to retailers to drive better on‑shelf availability.

Technology Investments to Turn Signals into Results

Spar said it has invested heavily in its technology stack, including cloud and ERP systems with Workday now stabilized. New workforce management tools, the SPARview mobile‑first data platform and AI‑based issue detection are designed to link real‑time store insights to measurable in‑store actions, supporting higher‑value services and better margins.

Margin Compression and Gross Profit Slide

Despite modest revenue growth, fiscal 2025 gross profit fell to $21.7 million, down from $33.6 million a year earlier, as gross margin dropped to 15.9% from 20.5%. Management cited a mix shift into higher‑cost remodel work, wage pressures and higher travel costs as key drivers, making margin recovery central to the turnaround plan.

Operating and Net Losses Deepen

The company swung to a large operating loss of $16.9 million in 2025 compared with a small $0.7 million operating profit in 2024. Net loss widened sharply to $24.6 million, or $1.04 per diluted share, versus a $3.2 million loss, or $0.13 per share, the prior year, reflecting both weaker margins and elevated restructuring‑related expenses.

Negative Adjusted EBITDA and Liquidity Strain

Consolidated adjusted EBITDA turned negative at $8.6 million, a roughly $15.3 million swing from positive $6.7 million in 2024, signaling that underlying earnings power deteriorated. Operating activities used $18.4 million of cash in 2025, leaving just $3.3 million in cash at year‑end and highlighting the importance of quickly reducing cash burn to ease near‑term liquidity pressure.

One‑Time Costs and Restructuring Weigh on Results

Management stressed that 2025 results were heavily burdened by nonrecurring items, including about $7.0 million of one‑time SG&A charges and out‑of‑period write‑offs. There were also $4.8 million of restructuring and severance costs, which together significantly depressed reported profitability as Spar reshaped its organization and footprint.

Quarterly Volatility and Fourth‑Quarter Weakness

Executives flagged notable quarterly volatility, with a pronounced fourth‑quarter “air pocket” marked by revenue decline and negative gross margins tied to project timing and mix. They reminded investors that Q4 is historically Spar’s weakest quarter and may continue to sit below full‑year margin targets, even if the annual outlook remains more constructive.

Service Mix and Margin Pressure

A pivot toward labor‑intensive, travel‑heavy remodel projects and ongoing wage inflation compressed margins throughout 2025. Looking ahead, Spar’s plan hinges on shifting its service mix toward higher‑margin merchandising and on‑demand work, using technology and partnerships to win more profitable assignments and lessen exposure to low‑margin projects.

Forward‑Looking Guidance and Turnaround Path

For fiscal 2026, management guided revenue to $143 million–$151 million, implying roughly 5%–11% growth over 2025, with gross margins improving to 20.5%–22.5%. They expect this rebound to be driven by a richer merchandising mix, technology‑enabled wallet expansion and SG&A normalizing to a $25.5 million–$26.5 million run rate, though quarterly volatility and the need to curb cash burn remain key execution risks.

Spar Group’s earnings call left investors weighing a credible but demanding turnaround story against a backdrop of steep recent losses and tight liquidity. The company has sharpened its focus, invested in technology and laid out clear margin and cost goals, but its stock narrative now hinges on proving that these strategic moves can quickly translate into sustainable cash‑generating growth.

data.title: Knightscope Earnings Call: Investment Now, Payoff Later?
data.description:
Knightscope Inc Class A ((KSCP)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Knightscope Inc.’s latest earnings call struck a cautious but constructive tone as management balanced modest revenue growth and a bolstered cash position against deepening losses and persistent supply chain headwinds. Executives framed 2025 as a foundational investment year, arguing that recent financing, a strategic acquisition and new platform development set the stage for potential acceleration in 2026–2027.

Modest revenue growth led by services

Knightscope posted approximately 4.9% full‑year revenue growth to $11.3 million, a modest uptick that underscored both opportunity and execution risk. Management highlighted that expansion was driven mainly by services, including Machine‑as‑a‑Service autonomous security robots and full‑service maintenance plans, signaling a tilt toward recurring, contract‑based income.

Acquisition pivots to full managed security

The acquisition of Event Risk, now rebranded as Knightscope Security Force, was presented as a strategic pivot toward selling full managed security solutions rather than stand‑alone hardware. Executives argued this combination of technology with human guards should unlock better cross‑sell potential and deeper penetration of what they described as a roughly $230 billion total addressable security market.

Balance sheet bolstered by $42.2 million raise

To support its scaled‑up ambitions, Knightscope raised roughly $42.2 million through financing in 2025, resulting in an estimated 83% year‑over‑year improvement in its cash position versus an $11.1 million reference base. Management stressed that this strengthened liquidity provides runway to continue investing in growth and integration efforts despite ongoing operating cash burn.

Heavy investment in new platforms and software

Research and development spending increased by about $5.4 million year‑over‑year as Knightscope doubled down on next‑generation platforms such as the K7, K1 Capsule and Super Tower, along with its Signals software platform. The company emphasized upcoming milestones including public showcases of new hardware and progress toward K7 beta testing, which are intended to enhance the product portfolio and competitive moat.

Security force stability defies industry churn

Management highlighted that Knightscope Security Force has grown to more than 400 employees while maintaining approximately 6% turnover, far below an industry range cited at 100%–400%. Board‑approved stock option programs are being used to recruit and retain personnel, and executives framed workforce stability as a competitive asset for delivering consistent managed services.

Healthy pipeline, but execution is now key

The sales funnel was described as “rather healthy,” with management emphasizing a strategic shift from selling single hardware units to bundled, end‑to‑end managed security solutions. The company stressed that integration of offerings, disciplined execution and converting this pipeline into recurring contracts will be the decisive factors in translating strategic progress into revenue growth.

Q4 revenue hit by supply chain constraints

Despite full‑year growth, fourth‑quarter 2025 revenue fell about 9.8% year‑over‑year, largely because supply chain disruptions delayed shipments of conducted energy device products. Management framed the decline as timing‑related rather than demand‑driven, but the shortfall spotlighted the operational fragility that can quickly impact quarterly performance.

Gross margin under pressure, quarterly gross loss

Knightscope reported a Q4 gross loss of around $1.6 million, citing elevated material costs and under‑absorption of fixed manufacturing overhead in a still‑subscale production environment. Executives acknowledged ongoing margin pressure and linked improvement prospects to achieving greater scale, stabilizing production and better balancing capacity with demand.

Net losses widen on higher spending

The company’s Q4 net loss widened to roughly $11 million, and full‑year net loss reached about $33.8 million, reflecting only modest revenue growth against heavier investment and weak gross margins. Management framed the red ink as the cost of building platforms, services and distribution necessary to pursue a large market, but investors will be watching for tangible progress toward narrowing those losses.

Operating expenses climb on R&D and SG&A

Fourth‑quarter operating expenses reached about $9.7 million, up roughly $3.8 million year‑over‑year as Knightscope boosted R&D and selling, general and administrative spending. For the full year, operating expenses rose approximately 12.1%, driven mainly by the $5.4 million R&D increase, partially offset by lower SG&A and the absence of prior restructuring charges.

Significant operating cash burn continues

Operating activities consumed around $30.3 million of cash in 2025, underscoring the company’s dependence on external financing to fund growth and development. While the recent capital raise provides short‑term breathing room, the business model’s sustainability ultimately hinges on driving higher‑margin revenue and tightening cost discipline.

Supply chain volatility remains a headwind

Management detailed persistent supply chain issues, including extended lead times, single‑source component risk and geopolitical and tariff impacts that constrained shipments and hurt unit economics. A new supply chain team, multi‑sourcing initiatives and inventory investments are expected to deliver somewhat better outcomes in 2026, but executives cautioned that many external factors remain beyond their control.

Improved loss per share still signals unprofitability

Weighted‑average loss per share for 2025 was approximately $4, which management noted represented a roughly 63.5% year‑over‑year improvement. Even so, they acknowledged that the figure underscores continued unprofitability, reinforcing the importance of scaling revenues and improving margins over the next several years.

Guidance and milestones point to 2026–2027 inflection

Looking ahead, Knightscope guided investors to expect continued supply‑chain noise in 2026 but modest operational improvements as mitigation efforts take hold. Key milestones include the immediate unveiling of the K1 Capsule and Super Tower, K7 beta testing in the second half of 2026, commercialization of the Signals platform, ongoing integration of Knightscope Security Force and a targeted acceleration in revenue and improving profit metrics into 2027.

Knightscope’s earnings call painted a picture of a company in transition, using fresh capital and a strategic acquisition to pivot into integrated managed security while absorbing sizable losses and operational volatility. For investors, the thesis now rests on whether management can execute on product rollouts, integration and pipeline conversion fast enough to validate the long‑term strategy before the cash cushion thins.

data.title: Loar Holdings Earnings Call Signals Profitable Growth
data.description:
Loar Holdings Inc. ((LOAR)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Loar Holdings Inc. struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, underscoring record revenue, record adjusted EBITDA and expanding margins despite higher interest costs and acquisition-related accounting noise. Management highlighted powerful cash generation, a deep new-product pipeline and active M&A, and even with near-term EPS pressure, boosted its outlook through 2026.

Record Revenue and Quarterly Performance

Loar reported record full-year sales of $500 million alongside a record fourth quarter, confirming strong momentum across its portfolio. Q4 sales rose 17% year over year, with various pro forma snapshots showing growth approaching or exceeding 20%, underscoring both organic strength and recent acquisition contributions.

Strong Adjusted EBITDA and Margin Expansion

Adjusted EBITDA reached a record $189 million for 2025, an increase of $43 million versus 2024, reflecting meaningful operating improvement. Fourth-quarter adjusted EBITDA grew by $10 million with a margin of 38.7%, and management is now openly targeting an ambitious roughly 40% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2026.

Improved Gross Profit and Operating Leverage

Gross profit margins moved sharply higher as Loar’s scale and mix shift kicked in, with Q4 gross margin up about 320 basis points from a year earlier. For the full year, gross margin rose to 52.7%, roughly 330 basis points better, driven by operating leverage, strategic pricing and a favorable mix toward higher-value content.

Commercial Aftermarket and OEM Growth

Commercial aerospace demand remained a core growth pillar, with aftermarket sales up 19% for the year and accelerating to 34% growth in Q4 as aging fleets and robust travel boosted parts demand. Commercial OEM sales climbed roughly 11% for the year and 8% in Q4, reflecting a healthier production backdrop from aircraft manufacturers.

Defense Sales Momentum

Defense revenue advanced 19% year over year and 14% in the fourth quarter, fueled by strong demand, new product introductions and market share gains across key platforms. Management sees defense as a continued growth driver supported by geopolitical dynamics, while stressing that order timing makes this business inherently uneven by quarter.

Exceptional Free Cash Flow Conversion

Cash generation was a standout, with free cash flow conversion of 138% for 2025 and an even stronger 160% after adjusting for a one-time tax benefit. With capital expenditures running at about 3% of sales and guided to roughly $19 million for 2026, Loar is converting earnings into cash at a rate that supports both debt reduction and acquisition firepower.

Robust M&A Activity and $600 Million Product Pipeline

Since going public, Loar has deployed more than $1.1 billion on acquisitions, effectively doubling its size, with LMB Fans & Motors and Harper Engineering among the latest additions. Management pointed to a new-product pipeline worth over $600 million in potential sales over five years and expects M&A to remain central, historically executing one to two deals a year.

2026 Upward Guidance and Financial Targets

The company raised its 2026 financial outlook, now projecting net sales of $640 million to $650 million and adjusted EBITDA of $253 million to $258 million, implying about a 40% margin. Net income is expected between $59 million and $63 million and adjusted EPS of $0.76 to $0.80, reflecting robust growth but also the impact of acquisition-related expenses.

Adjusted EPS Guidance Reduced Due to Acquisition Accounting

Despite higher revenue and EBITDA targets, Loar cut its adjusted EPS guidance to the $0.76 to $0.80 range, citing incremental non-cash depreciation and amortization tied to recent deals. Management emphasized that these charges are largely accounting in nature and should fade as synergies build, positioning earnings to better reflect the underlying cash economics over time.

Higher Interest Expense from Acquisition Funding

Financing the LMB and Harper acquisitions is set to push full-year interest expense to about $80 million, pressuring 2026 net income even as operations strengthen. Investors are being asked to look through this near-term drag, with management arguing that the acquired assets will scale and support higher EBITDA in future years.

Public Company and Integration Costs

Loar noted that ongoing public company obligations, including compliance and governance requirements, are now largely embedded in its 2025 run-rate cost structure. These expenses, along with one-time transaction and integration costs, have partly offset margin gains versus the pre-IPO era, but management believes they are manageable against the larger earnings base.

Defense Revenue Volatility

While defense growth has been impressive, the company reiterated that order patterns create a “lumpy” revenue profile that can skew quarterly comparisons. This volatility introduces forecasting risk for investors even as the long-term trajectory appears favorable, reinforcing the need to focus on multi-year trends rather than single quarters.

Acquisition-Related Margin Dilution Risk

Management acknowledged that acquisitions can temporarily dilute margins when bought businesses operate below the corporate average, raising execution risk. As Loar continues to lean on M&A for growth, the timing of synergies and integration success will be critical in translating deal activity into sustained margin and earnings expansion.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Growth Outlook

Looking ahead, Loar forecasts organic sales growth of more than 10% annually and adjusted EBITDA growth of at least 15%, with commercial OEM and aftermarket expected to grow at low double-digit rates and defense in the mid-single digits. The company is planning capital spending at roughly 3% of sales, while anticipating higher interest and non-cash charges, and expects recent acquisitions to become meaningfully accretive over the next several years.

Loar’s earnings call painted a picture of a high-margin aerospace supplier leaning into both organic growth and acquisitions, while navigating the short-term costs of expansion and public-company life. For investors, the key takeaway is a business delivering record results and stronger long-term guidance, but with near-term EPS muted by financing and accounting headwinds.

data.title: Steris Extends Former CFO Tokich’s Advisory Role
data.description:
Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

The latest update is out from Steris ( (STE) ).

On March 31, 2026, Steris plc amended a prior transition agreement with former Chief Financial Officer Michael J. Tokich, extending his post-executive employment as a part-time senior financial advisor from April 1, 2026, through March 31, 2027. During this extended advisory period, Tokich’s annual base salary will be reduced to $60,000, he may receive a discretionary cash bonus, and he will no longer be eligible for company health, dental, life insurance, vacation, disability or other employee benefit plans, while all other terms of the original transition agreement remain in effect.

data.title: Loma Negra Overhauls Board and Audit Committee After Mass Director Resignations
data.description:
Easter Sale - 70% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

An announcement from Loma Negra Compania Industrial Argentina Sociedad Anonima ( (LOMA) ) is now available.

On April 6, 2026, Loma Negra reported a sweeping change in its boardroom, disclosing the resignation of Chairman Paulo Sergio de Oliveira Diniz and five other directors, which left the board unable to function under the company’s bylaws. With no alternate directors available and urgent corporate decisions pending, the Supervisory Committee invoked powers under Argentine corporate law to accept the resignations and swiftly appoint six new full directors and three alternate directors to complete the current term.

The newly appointed directors include a mix of independent and non-independent members as defined by Argentine, SEC and NYSE rules, with Marcos Marcelo Mindlin named president and Sergio Damián Faifman as vice-president. That same day, the board reconstituted the Audit Committee with three independent members, designating Dario Gustavo Epstein as chairperson and recognizing him as an Audit Committee Financial Expert, moves that stabilize governance and aim to reassure investors about oversight and continuity after the abrupt leadership shift.

The most recent analyst rating on (LOMA) stock is a Hold with a $11.50 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on Loma Negra Compania Industrial Argentina Sociedad Anonima stock, see the LOMA Stock Forecast page.

Spark’s Take on LOMA Stock

According to Spark, TipRanks’ AI Analyst, LOMA is a Neutral.

The score is held back primarily by unstable profitability and the swing to negative free cash flow in 2025, despite a strong revenue rebound and a solid equity base. Limited technical data prevents confirming market momentum, and valuation is difficult to support with a negative P/E and no dividend yield.

To see Spark’s full report on LOMA stock, click here.

Limited, acquiring a 50% undivided co-ownership interest in the QIKBIM system and related IP for a total purchase price of $17.5 million, partly offset by $11,994,800 of historical payments. The buyer will pay the remaining consideration through a final installment and supplemental payments by December 31, 2026, with escrow, interest on late amounts, and no reversal of the assignment in case of delayed payment.

Net monetization proceeds from the QIKBIM IP will be shared 50/50 after recovery of buyer-funded development costs, with the buyer controlling invoicing and accounting and the seller providing 24 months of transition and technical support, reinforcing OFA Group’s control over the platform’s commercialization and long-term business strategy.
